text: The surgeon creates an epigastric port two centimeters below the lower border of the liver, ensuring it is directed towards the gallbladder. The assisting ports are positioned in the mid-clavicular and mid-axillary lines, maintaining triangulation with the camera port.

The surgeon uses an instrument to assess the gallbladder's position and adhesions, identifying the cystic duct and common bile duct junction. The midclavicular line port is placed 10 to 12 centimeters from the target organ, with the anterior axillary line port similarly positioned. The epigastric port is visible below the liver, facilitating ergonomic manipulation of the gallbladder with triangulated instruments.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the gallbladder, carefully separating flimsy adhesions. The duodenum is visible nearby, and the assistant provides traction to safely mobilize the gallbladder away from the duodenum.

The surgeon has divided all peritoneal attachments, safely pushing the duodenum away. The Ruvius sulcus is visible in the background, indicating proximity to the CBD. The surgeon begins scoring the peritoneum close to the gallbladder in a thin patient, with visible green and red structures depicting the CBD and hepatic artery course, ensuring work is done well away from these structures.

The surgeon scores the anterior peritoneum, visible in the foreground, as part of the cholecystectomy procedure. The cystic artery begins to appear in the surgical field, with the surgeon working medial to the artery, avoiding lateral dissection.

The surgeon performs peritoneal scoring medial to the artery, removing fatty tissue between the artery and duct. Moving posteriorly, the peritoneum is scored to connect spaces on both sides. A short burst of cautery on the peritoneum allows carbon dioxide to enter, opening the avascular plane for safe dissection.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to coagulate small vessels, followed by cutting current or desiccate mode for precise tissue coagulation. The lower end of the gallbladder is being mobilized from the cystic plate, ensuring a comprehensive critical view of safety. The dissection at the liver bed level is clearly visible.

The surgeon addresses the remaining attachments near the base of the cystic plate at the lower end of the gallbladder. Small vessels are coagulated using a hook, and if persistent bleeding occurs, a bipolar device is employed to securely coagulate oozing from the liver bed without causing further damage.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to dissect within the avascular plane near the lower third of the gallbladder, ensuring a clear critical view of safety by carefully following the tissue planes without fibrosis.

The surgeon works on the anterior side, dissecting medial to the cystic artery to elongate it for secure hemostasis. Increased traction is applied, and fatty tissue near the gallbladder is removed using a monopolar hook. A window is created to the posterior side, revealing the liver through the opening.

The surgeon uses a hook to divide fatty tissue near the vessel, widening the window on the anterior side. The peritoneum is gradually scored, revealing an avascular plane for safe dissection. The posterior dissection is also initiated, with two structures visible leading to the gallbladder.

The surgeon releases the peritoneum to improve the critical view of safety, continuing dissection on the liver bed to free the lower end of the gallbladder. Small fatty attachments are gradually released, and the peritoneum on the anterior side is dissected to ensure the porta hepatis is clear from the surgical area, leaving the gallbladder attached to the cystic plate.

The surgeon identifies two structures clearly visible as the gallbladder attachment. An atraumatic instrument is used to milk the cystic duct, ensuring any small stones are pushed back into the gallbladder. The assisting hand holds the duct while a medial large clip is applied to ligate the cystic duct. Due to the duct's width, a vial suture is used for ligation, ensuring secure closure.

The surgeon performs a surgeon's throw followed by two single throws to secure the knot. The gallbladder is held with the assisting hand, and an endo loop is applied to the proximal area to ensure the knot is secure, preventing bile spillage.

The surgeon divides the duct, causing it to fall laterally and downwards, confirming it is not the common bile duct (CBD). The gallbladder is then released from the cystic plate with careful traction, identifying the correct dissection planes. Small flimsy attachments are released without entering the liver capsule, minimizing bleeding. The surgeon works around the gallbladder, adjusting traction to stretch tissues and identify the avascular plane.

The surgeon changes traction to improve visibility and uses a monopolar hook to dissect flimsy attachments around the gallbladder. The hook is used with its belly for blunt dissection, minimizing trauma and avoiding entry into the liver bed. Small vessels are coagulated using the hook, ensuring clear vision by cleaning the tip to reduce fumes.

The surgeon adjusts traction and counter-traction to identify dissection planes around the gallbladder. The anterior portion is released, leaving only the posterior portion attached. The surgeon monitors the liver bed for bleeding while releasing small fibrous attachments.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to dissect around small vessels and peritoneum near the liver parenchyma, ensuring hemostasis by coagulating any small blood vessels. The liver bed is visible with a small blood clot that has been coagulated. The gallbladder is placed into an endo bag, and the bile is suctioned out, collapsing the gallbladder. The gallbladder, containing a large stone, is then removed through a small incision without tearing the endo bag.

The surgeon closes the tenamen ports using a port suture needle and administers a laparoscopic-guided TAP block, with the TA muscle visibly bulging. The critical view of safety is achieved, showing the cystic duct and artery clearly from the anterior view. Small peritoneal attachments are dissected, confirming only two structures are present, with the gallbladder positioned higher and the porta lower.

The surgeon demonstrates the doublet view, capturing both posterior and anterior perspectives to ensure only one duct and one artery are visible. The Ruvius sulcus is prominently visible, with the CBD located below it at the portal level. The dissection is performed above the Ruvius sulcus, close to the gallbladder, while scoring the peritoneum. A bulging structure identified as the lymph node of Lund is visible above the hook, guiding the dissection to remain above it. The surgeon corrects the dissection line to stay above the lymph node, ensuring the ligation of the artery is also above this landmark.

The surgeon continues dissection in the correct plane, revealing a distinct cleavage plane between the artery and gallbladder. Small attachments are released, and a tubular structure is visible, aiding in maintaining a critical view of safety.

The surgeon identifies the Ruvius sulcus on the left side of the screen, noting that the dissection is occurring below this landmark. A tubular structure is visible, identified as the Ruvius sulcus, with the gallbladder and cystic duct junction positioned higher. The portal vein is exposed, with the hepatic artery retracted by the right instrument and the common bile duct (CBD) visible on the left. The surgeon carefully dissects around the CBD, releasing fibrotic attachments and revealing the proximal portion of the hepatic duct.

The surgeon identifies the proximal course of the hepatic duct and the window between the cystic duct and hepatic duct. An abnormal anatomy is observed, with the artery resembling a Mohenian hump. The surgeon meticulously dissects, revealing the hepatic artery below the instrument and the hepatic duct beneath it. Small strands are coagulated under control.

The surgeon clearly visualizes the artery and the course of the cystic duct, with the hepatic duct visible below. The prominent vessel is noted post-division. In the case of cholecystitis, gauze is introduced to improve visibility amidst surrounding fat and inflamed tissues. Using a monopolar hook, the surgeon identifies the border of the gallbladder, noting small areas of bleeding.

The surgeon begins to visualize the gallbladder amidst surrounding fat and small vessels, which are oozing. The gallbladder is hugged and the peritoneum and fat surrounding it are released to clearly identify the gallbladder for precise dissection. The tubular structure of the gallbladder becomes visible as the peritoneum is incised anteriorly towards its attachment to the cystic plate. Significant inflammatory fluid is present, and traction and counter-traction are applied to separate inflammatory attachments.

The surgeon creates a window around the cystic duct, revealing the liver on the opposite side. Small attachments are carefully dissected strand by strand. The artery is exposed, and a window is opened around it. The elongated gallbladder is held midway, showing only one structure entering due to the wide duct, which is ligated using intracorporeal sutures.

The surgeon uses a needle holder to demonstrate the course of the common bile duct (CBD) and ligates the duct proximally, leaving a small cuff of tissue to prevent ligature slippage. Visible sludge is present in the cutting area, prompting milking of the duct to push remnants towards the gallbladder. Sharp and blunt dissection techniques are employed to identify tissue planes, followed by gallbladder removal and securing hemostasis using a bipolar instrument.

The surgeon encounters fibrotic adhesions and thick fat around the gallbladder due to delayed presentation. A precise sharp dissection is performed to identify the gallbladder along its entire course. The surgeon releases fatty attachments by staying close to the gallbladder, starting from the posterior area and moving to the anterior segment. The traction is adjusted by repositioning the toothed instrument from the fundus to midway on the gallbladder to improve access.

The surgeon uses a Maryland bipolar instrument to perform a combination of sharp and blunt dissection, coagulating small vessels to minimize thermal spread. A window is opened, revealing two structures: the artery and the cystic duct. The surgeon releases the lower end of the gallbladder from the cystic plate to establish the critical view of safety (CVS). The artery and cystic duct are clearly visible, with the cystic duct appearing elongated. The surgeon ligates the vessels and notes the dense adherence of the gallbladder to the liver bed, indicating a challenging dissection plane.

The surgeon employs a fundus-first technique to improve the dissection plane, connecting the medial and fundus-first dissections. Dense adhesions are lysed in a patient with previous laparotomy and acute cholecystitis. The gallbladder, noted for its thick wall and distension, is punctured with an obturator to suction inflammatory fluid. Using toothed forceps, the surgeon releases surrounding fat and isolates the duct, encircling it for identification.

The surgeon identifies a landmark and continues dissection, encountering bleeding from the partially coagulated cystic artery. A pulsatile bleed is visible, and gauze is applied with pressure to control the bleeding. Blood is suctioned to maintain clear visibility. The duct is ligated, and the gallbladder is being released from the liver bed.

The surgeon encounters thick peritoneal attachments and inadvertently enters the gallbladder, revealing a visible stone. The gallbladder wall is held and dissected using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques, allowing it to peel off easily due to severe inflammation and infection.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the cystic plate, which appears irregular, avoiding accidental entry into the liver parenchyma. Inflammatory adhesions are visible, with bile extravasation observed. Using blunt atraumatic dissection with a suction apparatus, the adhesions are peeled off easily, showing surgical progress.

The surgeon encounters severe inflammation near the gallbladder, with the omentum adhered to the area. A large vessel is identified and used as a landmark for adhesiolysis, working above and lateral to it. A window is created, and the wide cystic duct is ligated. An intraoperative cholangiogram is performed to check for any remaining stones due to the failed ERCP.

The surgeon ligates the cystic duct and inserts a small infant catheter through it, administering a wash and injecting dye. Using a C-arm, the dye flow is observed, clearly visualizing the intrahepatic ducts and complete common bile duct, with spillage into the duodenum. The cystic duct junction is divided, and an endo loop is applied to both proximal and distal areas. A large stone is crushed inside an endo bag to facilitate removal, avoiding the need to enlarge the port incision. Dense fibrotic attachments around the gallbladder are visible, presenting difficulty in manipulation.

The surgeon encounters frank pus in the gallbladder due to acute cholecystitis and performs adhesiolysis. The gallbladder appears thickened, but the adhesions are not dense, allowing for easy separation using atraumatic dissection and suction. The gallbladder is identified more clearly, and a puncture needle is used to aspirate its contents.

The surgeon is unable to achieve a clear critical view of safety and decides to leave a cuff of tissue at the gallbladder-cystic duct junction. The gallbladder is incised at this level using a monopolar hook. The inside of the gallbladder appears sloughed off due to infection. A plane is created between the gallbladder and the cystic plate to carefully release adhesions.

The surgeon releases the last attachments of the gallbladder, revealing sloughed off tissue inside. This completes the disconnection of the gallbladder, allowing for a safe cholecystectomy without risk of bile duct injury.

The surgeon disconnects the gallbladder up to the fundus, leaving it partially attached for traction purposes. The infected and sloughed material is visible in the liver bed. Using bipolar coagulation, the surgeon prepares the healthy mucosa for suturing. The tissue is trimmed further, and three sutures are placed in the remnant segment, which appears larger due to laparoscopic magnification.

The surgeon uses a varus needle to aspirate the gallbladder, causing it to collapse visibly, which allows for easier manipulation of its thickened wall. The suction device points to the area of the common bile duct (CBD). The clip also includes a brief demonstration of CBD exploration, typically performed in patients with failed ERCP.

The surgeon releases adhesions starting from the cystic duct, creating a clear critical view of safety (CVS) in the area. The dissection progresses inferiorly towards the duodenum, with the common bile duct (CBD) gradually becoming visible.

The surgeon meticulously dissects inflammatory adhesions using cautery, sharp, and blunt techniques, revealing the duodenum lateral to the instrument. The bile duct appears distended and wide due to obstructing stones. The gallbladder is fully exposed up to the duodenum level. A balloon is inserted into the dilated duct for trans-cystic flushing and stone retrieval, but no major stones are visible, leading to a small choledochotomy.

The surgeon performs a sharp dissection during a choledochotomy, avoiding the use of electrocautery. A balloon is inserted into the duct to flush out sludge, which is visibly expelled. The balloon is inflated and retrieved to check for additional sludge or stones, with more sludge material being removed from the area.
The surgeon places a small infant feeding tube as a stent within the choledochotomy site and sutures the choledochotomy over the stent in a continuous fashion, ensuring the duct remains open.

The surgeon performs a choledochoduodenostomy by making a linear choledochotomy along the length of the common bile duct (CBD) and a corresponding incision on the duodenum. The first sutures are placed at the nine o'clock and six o'clock positions on the duodenum using a monofilament PDS suture to ensure a wide open anastomosis. The posterior and anterior bites are taken precisely under laparoscopic vision, completing the anastomosis without involving the posterior wall.

The surgeon observes the duodenum moving smoothly near the surgical site. The gallbladder remains attached to facilitate identification of the choledochal cyst and common bile duct (CBD) area. An endo loop is applied at the junction of these structures, ensuring clear visualization and control.

The surgeon addresses bleeding from the liver and an inflammatory area by initially applying gauze, which fails to stop the oozing. An abgel is then placed in the area, successfully halting the bleeding.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection on densely adherent fibrotic tissues, inadvertently injuring the duodenum. The duodenal mucosa is visibly open, with bile leaking from the duodenum. A balloon is inserted through the trans cystic area, emerging through the iatrogenic injury in the duodenum. The surgical field is washed, with fluid visibly flowing through the duodenum.

The surgeon inserts a small double J stent into the common bile duct (CBD) and applies a clip over it. The opening is sutured using a PDS suture, with the surgeon taking multiple bites to secure the closure. A small portion of the gallbladder, resembling a thimble, is visible after dissection, indicating fibrotic adhesions. The surgeon performs a pexy with the omentum to stabilize the area.

The surgeon dissects the remnant gallbladder, revealing its small size and adherence to the common bile duct (CBD) due to Mirizzi syndrome. Bile is observed flowing from a small rent in the CBD area. The gallbladder is released completely, leaving a portion near the liver for manipulation. The surgeon sutures the rent in the CBD with multiple interrupted stitches, ensuring no bile leak is present. The posterior wall of the gallbladder remains intact as the rest is removed, with a dry gauze used to confirm the absence of bile leakage.
